Name: curvature_aware_mixed_precision_quantization
Title: Curvature-Aware Adaptive Mixed-Precision Quantization: A Training-Free Approach
Short Hypothesis: Can curvature-based metrics provide a deterministic framework for adaptive mixed-precision quantization that outperforms heuristic-based methods while maintaining computational efficiency?
Related Work: Current methods, such as EMQ and InfoQ, focus on adaptive heuristics for mixed-precision quantization but often rely on extensive training or heuristics that lack rigorous mathematical grounding. Our approach integrates curvature-aware metrics to define a deterministic framework for bit-width allocation, distinguishing it from existing literature by emphasizing formalized quantization strategies without extensive training.
Abstract: In this proposal, we introduce a curvature-aware adaptive mixed-precision quantization framework that enables training-free bit-width allocation for neural networks. By leveraging curvature metrics obtained from Hessians and gradient covariance, we aim to establish a deterministic routing protocol for quantization that directly quantifies sensitivity to quantization errors across layers. Our approach formulates the quantization problem as an optimization task, utilizing curvature-aware diagnostics to allocate bit-widths in a manner that minimizes the degradation of model performance while ensuring computational efficiency. We will construct a comprehensive evaluation pipeline to validate our method against existing heuristic-driven approaches, measuring accuracy, computational overhead, and stability across various architectures. We anticipate that our framework will provide a novel perspective on MPQ, leading to improved operational efficiency for neural network deployment in resource-constrained environments.
Experiments: 1. Establish a baseline mixed-precision quantization framework using existing heuristic methods.
2. Implement the curvature-aware metrics into a new quantization algorithm that includes a deterministic routing protocol for bit-width allocation.
3. Conduct experiments on standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) comparing model accuracy, computational overhead, and memory usage between the proposed method and heuristic-based approaches.
4. Evaluate the stability of the model performance under varying quantization levels, measuring the sensitivity to curvature-based diagnostics and correlating with model accuracy.
Metrics for evaluation will include accuracy drop, computational overhead (FLOPs), and memory usage comparisons.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The reliance on curvature metrics may computationally increase the overhead during the initial evaluation phase.
Potential limitations in the generalizability of the framework across all neural network architectures, particularly those with unconventional training dynamics.
The effectiveness of the curvature metrics may vary based on the architecture and dataset, necessitating further empirical validation.
